questions.question: What is the primary benefit of Concurrent Rendering introduced in React 18?
options: It compiles JSX directly to WebAssembly for faster execution
It allows React to pause rendering heavy component trees to keep the browser main thread responsive to user input
It fetches all application data before rendering begins
It removes the need for state management libraries
questions.answer: It allows React to pause rendering heavy component trees to keep the browser main thread responsive to user input
questions.explanation: Concurrent rendering means React's renders are no longer blocking. React can break up the work, yielding to the browser so user inputs (typing, clicking) remain smooth even during heavy render tasks.

questions.question: How does useTransition differ from standard useState updates?
options: It updates CSS transition animations automatically
It marks the state update as non-urgent, allowing urgent updates (like typing) to interrupt the render
It defers the state update until the component is unmounted
It makes state updates run on a separate Web Worker
questions.answer: It marks the state update as non-urgent, allowing urgent updates (like typing) to interrupt the render
questions.explanation: startTransition tells React that the update inside it is low priority. If the user types into an input, React will pause rendering the transition to instantly update the input field.

questions.question: When using useDeferredValue to optimize a heavy component, what MUST you do to the heavy component?
options: Wrap the component in React.lazy()
Wrap the component in React.memo()
Add a unique key to the component
Convert the component to a class component
questions.explanation: useDeferredValue only helps if the heavy child component skips rendering when its props don't change. Without React.memo, the parent rendering the urgent value will force the child to re-render anyway.

questions.question: What behavior changed with Automatic Batching in React 18?
options: React now batches multiple API requests into a single HTTP call
React batches state updates inside asynchronous code like setTimeout and Promises into a single render
React batches CSS class updates to prevent layout thrashing
React requires you to call ReactDOM.unstable_batchedUpdates
questions.answer: React batches state updates inside asynchronous code like setTimeout and Promises into a single render
questions.explanation: Previously, updating three state variables inside a fetch() .then() block would cause three re-renders. In React 18, they are batched together automatically, resulting in just one re-render.

questions.question: What does the 'isPending' boolean returned from useTransition indicate?
options: Network data is still fetching
The Suspense boundary is open
React is actively rendering the non-urgent transition state in the background
The component is waiting to be mounted into the DOM
questions.answer: React is actively rendering the non-urgent transition state in the background
questions.explanation: isPending is true while React is processing the heavy update in the background. It is commonly used to show a loading spinner or lower the opacity of the old content.
